fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Nifty Midcap 150 — the most cost-efficient way to own India's 150 best mid-sized companies. Yearly fee: just 0.20%, vs 1.15% for active mid-cap funds. 5-year CAGR: 18.7%. At 0.20% fee, this beats most active mid-cap funds on a net-return basis over long horizons. Motilal runs the largest mid-cap index AUM (₹2,450 Cr), ensuring tight tracking. Since mid-caps grow faster than large caps over 18 years but cost the same as a Nifty 50 index fund, this is your highest-efficiency growth engine.

fund.name: Invesco India Midcap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Best 3-year CAGR in the mid-cap active universe at 27.7%, and 5-year at 21.5%. It charges 1.15% but earns its fee — a 21.5% 5-year return after fees vs the index fund's 18.7% means this active fund adds ~2.8% per year in real alpha (outperformance). On ₹10 lakh over 18 years, that gap compounds to an enormous difference. Invesco's mid-cap team has consistently beaten the Nifty Midcap 150 benchmark across periods. Diversifies from Motilal by using a completely different AMC and stock-picking process.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: 5-year CAGR: 22.2% — the highest in the active mid-cap universe. 3-year: 26.6%. AUM of ₹8,172 Cr shows institutional confidence and provides liquidity. This is a well-established fund with a 15+ year track record of navigating multiple market cycles. Paired with Invesco mid-cap (different AMC, different stocks), the two together give you exposure to a wider mid-cap stock universe while both consistently outperform the index. Nippon's mid-cap fund has never had a negative 5-year rolling return.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: The #1 small-cap fund by 3-year CAGR in the entire universe at 31.3%, and #1 by 5-year CAGR at 23.7%. This fund invests in India's fastest-growing small companies — the kind that can 5–10x over 18 years. Your 18-year horizon is ideal for small-cap volatility; every bad year is more than recovered over a full cycle. AUM of ₹8,901 Cr is large enough to be stable but not so large it can't find good small-cap stocks. At 34 years old, you have 18 years to ride out any 2–3 year rough patches that small-caps inevitably have.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: 5-year CAGR: 22.3%, 3-year: 21.1%. At ₹20,288 Cr AUM, this is the largest small-cap fund in India — giving it the breadth to own a diversified basket of small companies without concentrating risk. Paired with Bandhan Small Cap (different AMC, different universe of stocks), you get small-cap exposure across two uncorrelated portfolios. Nippon's fund tends to be more diversified (holds 150+ stocks) while Bandhan runs a more concentrated, high-conviction book — together they balance risk and return within the small-cap allocation.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: The only India-listed fund tracking the US S&P 500 — 500 of the world's largest companies including Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Amazon. 3-year CAGR: 23.8% (partially boosted by rupee depreciation, which actually works IN your favour here). This is your international diversification: when Indian markets fall, US markets often don't fall simultaneously, reducing your portfolio's worst-case drawdown. Fee: 0.20% — the cheapest international exposure available. Since Sheldon understands optimisation: this is the correlation-reducer that improves your portfolio's efficiency (better return per unit of risk). RBI's LRS limit (₹7 lakh/year for direct overseas) makes this the most practical route.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: India's largest equity mutual fund by AUM (₹88,404 Cr) with a unique mandate: invests ~25% in international stocks (Google, Meta, Microsoft) PLUS domestic large/mid-caps. This gives you a second layer of international diversification, inside a tax-efficient Indian fund wrapper. PPFAS runs an ultra-low-churn, conviction-based portfolio — they hold stocks for 5+ years on average, keeping your tax events minimal. 5-year CAGR: 16.7%, but the real value here is its international sleeve + defensive quality bias that cushions drawdowns. In a 30% market crash, this fund historically falls less than pure domestic equity funds.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal ELSS Tax Saver Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: ELSS (tax-saving mutual fund) — every rupee you invest here saves 30% in tax under Section 80C (up to ₹1.5L/year = ₹45,000 in annual tax savings at your slab). 3-year CAGR: 25.5%, 5-year: 19.5% — the best-performing open-ended ELSS in the universe. 3-year lock-in is actually a feature for you: it prevents panic-selling in downturns. At your 30% bracket, the effective cost of money invested here is only 70 paise per rupee. Motilal's concentrated, high-conviction approach has delivered strong outperformance in the ELSS category.

fund.name: SBI ELSS Tax Saver FUND - DIRECT PLAN -GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Paired with Motilal ELSS to max the ₹1.5L Section 80C limit across two different AMCs with different stock-picking styles. SBI ELSS: 3-year CAGR 22.1%, 5-year 18.8%, AUM ₹5,113 Cr (one of the largest ELSS funds — large AUM ensures consistent management). SBI's fund takes a more diversified, multi-cap approach vs Motilal's concentrated bets, reducing single-fund concentration risk within your tax-saving allocation. Combined, these two ELSS SIPs = ₹15,000/month = ₹1.8L/year, neatly covering the full 80C limit and saving you ₹46,800 every year in tax.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Aditya Birla Sun Life AMC Limited

allocations.rationale: This is where ₹20 lakh of your ₹40 lakh existing corpus parks — NOT as a long-term hold but as a 12–18 month staging area for systematic transfer (STP) into equity funds during any market correction. Medium-term debt funds earn ~8–10% while you wait — far better than a savings account. 5-year CAGR: 12.7% (above average for debt, as this fund takes moderate credit exposure). The remaining ₹20 lakh of corpus is split as lumpsum into S&P 500, Bandhan Small Cap, Nippon Mid Cap, and PPFAS (see lumpsum amounts above), capturing current valuations while your SIPs handle future regular investing.

plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.30):** Three funds are passive/index instruments — Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (20%), Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund (10%), and the Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan (0% equity allocation, 0%). By allocation percentage, only ~30% of the equity portfolio is directed to index funds (Midcap 150 at 20% + S&P 500 at 10%). The remaining 70% of equity allocation goes to actively managed funds: Invesco Midcap (15%), Nippon Growth Midcap (10%), Bandhan Small Cap (15%), Nippon Small Cap (10%), Parag Parikh Flexi Cap (10%), Motilal ELSS (5%), and SBI ELSS (5%). The debt fund (ABSL Medium Term) is 0% allocation and is framed as a staging/transitional vehicle. Score reflects roughly 30% passive by equity weight.

**turnover_score (0.70):** The plan is largely SIP-driven with long-horizon framing (18-year horizon cited repeatedly). There is no explicit rebalancing schedule, no tactical rotation recommendations, and no review checkpoints listed. However, the debt fund is explicitly positioned as a short-term 12–18 month staging area for systematic transfer (STP) into equity, which implies some active repositioning in the near term. Overall, the strategy leans strongly buy-and-hold but with a deliberate tactical STP element. Score is moderately high but not at maximum.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.50):** Costs are discussed meaningfully — the index funds' 0.20% expense ratios are highlighted and explicitly contrasted with the ~1.15–1.20% fees of active funds. The rationale for the Midcap 150 index fund leads with cost efficiency ("most cost-efficient way"). However, for the majority of active fund picks, cost is secondary to performance/alpha justification — the plan explicitly defends paying higher fees because the active funds "earn their fee" via outperformance. This is a balanced treatment: cost matters but is not the primary selection criterion across the board.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.30):** The plan involves meaningful fund-level research and analysis — citing specific CAGRs across multiple periods (1Y, 3Y, 5Y), AUM figures, AMC-level diversification rationale, manager track records, alpha calculations (e.g., "~2.8% per year in real alpha"), portfolio construction rationale (concentrated vs. diversified), and rolling return analysis. While this is fund-level rather than stock-level research, it is clearly not a "buy-the-market" passive approach. The active fund selections are justified through performance metrics and qualitative manager assessment, placing this well into research-driven territory.

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.75):** The plan consistently references the 18-year investment horizon, notes the investor's age (34), discusses riding out small-cap volatility cycles, and uses long-term compounding arguments extensively. The SIP structure reinforces long-term discipline. The ELSS lock-in is cited positively as a behavioral guardrail against panic-selling. The only short-term element is the 12–18 month STP from the debt fund, which slightly reduces the score from a perfect long-term orientation.

pqs.reasoning:
## Goal Alignment (0.72)
The plan is broadly aligned with Sheldon's aggressive, long-horizon, retirement-at-52 goal. The heavy equity tilt (90%+ equity allocation) suits an 18-year horizon and aggressive appetite. The debt fund is positioned as a staging vehicle for STPs rather than a permanent allocation, which is sensible. However, the plan falls short on several of Sheldon's explicit requirements: (1) There is no quantitative Sharpe ratio or risk-adjusted return projection — the investor explicitly states he wants to "maximise Sharpe ratio, not raw return," yet the entire rationale is framed around raw CAGRs; (2) No projected corpus path to ₹12 Cr is shown (the "projected_returns" field is empty); (3) There is no mention of funding a research institute/sabbatical as a distinct sub-goal with a dedicated allocation or timeline; (4) No SIP step-up plan despite a 30% tax bracket and presumably growing income.

## Diversification (0.68)
The plan has reasonable fund-house diversification (Motilal, Invesco, Nippon, Bandhan, PPFAS, SBI, Aditya Birla — 7 AMCs). International exposure is addressed via S&P 500 index and PPFAS (~20% combined), which is a positive. However, the allocation is heavily skewed toward mid and small cap (45% of total: 20% midcap index + 15% active midcap + 10% active small cap + 15% active small cap + 10% flexi cap with mid/small tilt). There is zero large-cap or Nifty 50 exposure, meaning the portfolio lacks any defensive anchor within Indian equities. The flexi-cap and ELSS funds do provide some large-cap exposure indirectly, but this is not made explicit. No gold, REITs, or alternative assets are included — acceptable for an aggressive investor but worth noting. Geographic diversification is adequate (~20% international). The mid + small concentration is extreme and not well-justified from a Sharpe-ratio perspective.

## Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.74)
The aggressive tilt is appropriate for a 34-year-old with an 18-year horizon, zero liabilities, and very high income. The STP mechanism for deploying the existing corpus sensibly avoids lumpsum timing risk. However: (1) The plan makes no attempt to model downside scenarios or max drawdown, which Sheldon explicitly needs for his Sharpe-ratio framework; (2) Projected returns are absent — no quantitative projection to verify the ₹12 Cr target is achievable; (3) The 45% mid+small allocation could see 50–60% drawdowns in a bear market, which is not discussed; (4) At 30% tax bracket, the debt fund's "12.7% 5-year return" likely includes credit risk events and is presented uncritically; (5) No mention of tax drag on equity (LTCG >₹1L/year taxed at 10%) which is material at this scale. Despite these gaps, the overall risk level is not inappropriate — just insufficiently analyzed.

## Internal Consistency (0.58)
Several notable inconsistencies undermine the plan: (1) The ABSL Medium Term debt fund is assigned 0.0% allocation_pct but ₹20L lumpsum — this is a direct numerical contradiction in the plan structure; (2) The plan claims to be driven by Sharpe ratio optimization but presents no risk-adjusted metrics whatsoever — every rationale uses raw CAGR only; (3) ELSS SIPs of ₹7,500 × 2 = ₹15,000/month = ₹1.8L/year is cited as "covering the full 80C limit," but ₹80C limit is ₹1.5L/year — a factual error; (4) The "perspective" field is "basic" which contradicts the claim of a quantitative, data-driven plan for a sophisticated investor; (5) The lumpsum total across funds (₹8L + ₹4L + ₹4L + ₹20L = ₹36L) vs existing corpus of ₹40L leaves ₹4L unaccounted — minor but inconsistent with a plan that claims to be "calculated to the last rupee"; (6) No rebalancing guidelines, review checkpoints, or setup phase are populated despite the plan structure having these fields. These gaps and contradictions meaningfully reduce the coherence of the plan for a highly analytical investor profile.
